title: Google Teachable Machine
description: This tool provides a simple interface for creating machine learning models without requiring coding experience. Setup time is minimal, making it suitable for introductory AI exploration.

• Compare model performance using different quantities of training samples (few vs many).
  • Adjust parameters such as epochs, batch size, and learning rate to observe their impact on accuracy and loss.
  • Investigate dataset bias or imbalance (e.g., training on only one color of object may reduce generalization).
  • Test model robustness by introducing noise into image or audio inputs.

This tool is well suited for introductory machine learning lessons and supports a wide range of classroom experiments focused on data, training, and model behavior. Project files can be opened through the platform using the built-in import feature.

title: Introduction to AI-Assisted Coding
description: This activity explores AI-assisted programming through the recreation of a Connections-style game. The original online IDE environment was unavailable, so the assignment was rebuilt as a standalone web project.

The exercise is designed to support learning in prompt engineering and code analysis. A structured approach can be used where students generate code using AI once, then focus on debugging and improving the output independently.

Two prompt variations are included in the download folder for comparison: a simple prompt and a detailed prompt. The outputs were analyzed to evaluate differences in structure, completeness, and required debugging effort.
This activity is suitable for both beginner and intermediate learners and can also serve as an introduction to web development concepts and AI-assisted workflow design.

title: Interactive Transformer Visualization of GPT-2
description: This tool visualizes how transformer-based language models generate text one token at a time, illustrating the step-by-step prediction process.
The interface includes explanatory text that describes how the model processes input and produces output. The main adjustable parameter is temperature, which controls randomness in token selection.
Suggested classroom applications include converting the explanation into guided slides or having students construct sentences while observing model behavior.

Additional resources include a 'Tokenizer Visualizer' and a lecture on transformers which provide further insight into model structure and token processing. Included files consist of screenshots of these tools.

title: Artificial Neuron
description: This guided activity introduces the concept of artificial neurons through interactive explanations and embedded quizzes. It focuses on foundational machine learning concepts such as inputs, weights, and bias.
The activity is designed to support conceptual understanding rather than advanced technical implementation, making it suitable for introductory learners.
Assessment can be supported through documentation or screenshot-based submissions of completed activities.
Supplementary materials include screenshots of the activity and related external resources that reinforce the concept of neural computation.

This website on 'How to Think Like a Neuron' is similar to this activity. It provides an additional perspective on neural network concepts and reinforces understanding of how artificial neurons process information.

title: API Explorer - Class Activity
description: This interactive activity introduces students to working with APIs using JavaScript fetch requests. It provides a sandbox environment where users can modify API calls and observe real-time JSON responses.

• Practice using query parameters to filter and sort data (e.g., category, platform, popularity).
  • Learn how to retrieve a single resource using path parameters (e.g., fetching a game by ID).
  • Understand how API keys work by testing requests with and without authentication.
  • Explore error handling by comparing valid and invalid requests and analyzing status codes.

The activity is well suited for introductory web development or computer science courses and reinforces core concepts such as client-server communication, JSON data structures, and debugging API responses.

title: Godot 4 Multiplayer Tutorial
description: This tutorial demonstrates how to create a basic 3D multiplayer game in Godot 4 using built-in networking tools. It walks through setting up a host and client system, spawning players, and synchronizing gameplay across multiple instances.

• Host and join system using ENet multiplayer peer.
  • Player spawning with MultiplayerSpawner.
  • RPCs for syncing and removing players.
  • Position and rotation sync with MultiplayerSynchronizer.
This tutorial is best suited for intermediate users who already understand basic Godot concepts and want to learn how multiplayer networking works..

title: Intro to Computer Science
description: A self-paced introductory course covering the fundamentals of computer science and programming.

• Binary Module: How computers store and process data using 0s and 1s.
  • Logic Module: How computers use boolean values to make decisions.
  • Hardware Module: evolution of computers and how software runs on physical systems.
Designed for complete beginners with no prior experience, introducing core concepts that underpin all modern computing.
